exemplar.title:
(Biology, Economics) / To what extent does climate change, urbanization and
sanitation impact the spread and hence the economic burden of Dengue Virus
in Brazil?

detailedExemplarData.title:
(Biology, Economics) / To what extent does climate change, urbanization and
sanitation impact the spread and hence the economic burden of Dengue Virus
in Brazil?

strengths: Clear identification of a geographical focus (Minas Gerais) and three explanatory variables
Explicit statement of the research question and outline of quantitative methodology
Appropriate selection of secondary data sources with cross-tabulation methods described
weaknesses: Research question scope is too broad (refers to âBrazilâ rather than state-level) and bundles multiple drivers
Methodology justification is brief, lacking rationale for specific tests and cost-estimate choices
Conclusions restate findings without explicitly tying back to the refined research question
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work sits in the upper half of the 3â4 band: it communicates topic and question clearly and shows generally appropriate methods, but focus remains only partially sharp and methodological justification is under-developed. A score of 4 reflects these strengths and limitations.

strengths: Use of over forty reputable scientific, governmental and economic sources directly linked to variables
Clear and coherent demonstration of virology (DENV structure, ADE) and economic models (PPC, AD/AS)
Mostly accurate use of technical terminology from both biology and economics
weaknesses: Some duplicate or marginal sources reduce overall relevance
Occasional spelling errors (âDenqueâ) and inconsistent mathematical notation
Basic definitions remain at an introductory level without deeper conceptual integration
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Knowledge is well communicated and integrated (upper 3â4 band) but lacks the expert-level source selection and consistently precise terminology needed for a 5â6. A score of 4 balances substantial strengths with noted inconsistencies.

strengths: Data sets are rigorously selected from WHO, DATASUS, INMET, IBGE and FIEMG and align closely with research variables
Analysis is coherent, supported by tables and graphs, with logical interdisciplinary links between biology and economics
Argument follows a clear structure, demonstrating multiple lines of evidence and critical insight into GDP losses and transmission dynamics
weaknesses: Statistical rigor is uneven (no p-values, confidence intervals or sensitivity analyses)
Depth of evaluation varies; some causal claims lack sufficient evidence
Limitations and counter-arguments are noted but not fully developed
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The submission falls solidly within the 7â9 band: research selection is robust, analysis generally coherent, and argument structured with critical insight. However, uneven depth and limited statistical evaluation preclude reaching the top band. An 8 appropriately rewards its strengths while acknowledging areas for deeper critique.

strengths: Clear academic structure with contents page, numbered headings and logical chapters
Integration of maps, graphs and schematics that generally aid comprehension
Comprehensive bibliography with in-text citations present
weaknesses: Visuals suffer from low resolution and missing labels or scale bars
Referencing style is inconsistent (mix of Harvard, footnotes, raw URLs) and page numbering is absent
Tables and figures occasionally lack axis labels and clear captions
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Presentation meets the 3â4 band in overall structure and use of visuals but is hampered by multiple formatting inconsistencies and resolution issues. A score of 3 reflects its coherent form yet noticeable weaknesses.

weaknesses: No RPPF entries or reflective commentary provided
No evidence of personal engagement, planning choices or future research proposals
Intellectual initiative and reflective insight are absent
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: With no reflective evidence or RPPF extracts, the work cannot demonstrate the required engagement. A score of 0 is therefore assigned.

strandSelections.reasoning: The essay follows a clear progression from biology to economics and uses diagrams to structure reasoning. Interdisciplinary links are present but not deeply critiqued; counter-arguments and limitations receive little attention. Thus the argument is structured with âsome critical insightâ, aligning with a mid-band score.

A comprehensive bibliography is supplied, and in-text citations appear, yet formatting varies (Harvard, footnotes, raw URLs) and several references repeat. Page numbering is absent in the PDF view. Given these inconsistencies, the strand meets but does not exceed the basic standard.

The title and introductory section convey a clear geographical focus (Minas Gerais) and identify three explanatory variables. Nevertheless, the scope still stretches across both national data and stateâlevel analysis and combines public-health and macro-economic impacts, so the exact centre of gravity is only partially sharp. Overall clarity is good but not exemplary, fitting the 3-4 band.

A contents page, numbered headings and logical chapters give the report a recognisable academic form. Minor inconsistencies (duplicated section numbers, occasional run-on tables) impede full coherence, so the work earns the lower half of the top band.

The essay demonstrates solid biological understanding of DENV structure, transmission and ADE and applies basic economic models (PPC, AD/AS). These concepts are linked to Minas Gerais data with moderate effectiveness. Some causal leaps and un-qualified assumptions remain, so knowledge is âclear and integratedâ but not consistently incisive.
No RPPF extracts or other reflective commentary were provided, so there is no evidence on which to evaluate the quality of reflections.

The student correlates climatic anomalies with outbreak maps and calculates GDP losses. Explanations often follow logically and are supported by tables/graphs, but some claims (e.g., poverty cycle forecasts) are asserted with limited evidence. Coherence is good, yet depth of evaluation is uneven, matching the centre of the 4-6 band.

The research question is explicitly stated, but it refers to âBrazilâ while most evidence concerns Minas Gerais, and it bundles several drivers and two dependent variables into one sentence. This gives a recognisable but only partially focused question, consistent with the mid-band.

Data sets from WHO, DATASUS, INMET, IBGE and FIEMG are well aligned to the research variables and span 2018-2024, indicating deliberate selection. A few sources (e.g., media articles) add little analytical value, so rigour is slightly below the top band but comfortably within 7-9.
The submission includes no evidence of planning choices, self-direction or future research proposals in the RPPF; therefore intellectual initiative cannot be credited.

The student outlines reliance on secondary quantitative sources and describes how climatic, urbanisation and economic data are cross-tabulated. Source selection is generally appropriate, yet the justification is brief (e.g., no rationale for correlation tests or choice of cost estimates) and primary data limitations are only superficially addressed. This corresponds to âgenerally appropriateâ methods with some informed selection.

More than forty reputable scientific, governmental and economic databases are referenced and most link directly to the variables examined. Although some duplicate or marginal sources appear, the majority are suitable and varied, meriting the upper end of the 3-4 band rather than expert-level selection.
Without the required RPPF entries, personal engagement with the research process cannot be demonstrated and must be recorded as absent.

Maps, graphs and schematics are integrated and usually captioned. Some images are low-resolution and a few lack full source details, but overall they aid comprehension and are correctly placed, fitting the 3-4 band.

Technical terms from virology and macro-economics are mostly correct (e.g., antibody-dependent enhancement, LRAS) and used repeatedly. Occasional spelling errors ("Denque", "emplovability") and inconsistent symbol use show the terminology is accurate but not consistently precise, placing it in the middle of the 3-4 band.

annotations.comment: Definitions of viral structure are accurate but remain basic; integrate deeper virology concepts (e.g. receptor specificity) to showcase advanced understanding.
Figure 1 caption omits source details and resolution info; include full citation and ensure image quality supports analysis.
The antibody-dependent enhancement discussion ends mid-sentence without concluding its implications; ensure full explanation for clarity.
The title is clear and appropriately communicates the focus on dengue in Minas Gerais, integrating both biological and economic lenses.
The research question refers broadly to âBrazilâ while your evidence is state-level (Minas Gerais) and bundles multiple variables; narrow the scope and simplify wording for full focus.
You meet the word-count requirement by stating 3950 words, satisfying the formatting criterion.
The table of contents lacks consistent page references for each section; include accurate page numbers to improve navigation.
Your introduction provides solid global context on dengue risk and vector biology, demonstrating clear knowledge application.
Table 1âs header and row labels are ambiguous; ensure region and death counts are clearly distinguished and align cells properly.
The spelling âDenqueâ appears repeatedly; correct to âDengueâ to maintain scientific accuracy and consistent use of terminology.

The use of mathematical symbols (e.g. \(
\blacktriangledown
\) over years) is inconsistent and confusing; standardize notation for clearer data presentation.
Footnotes on page 5 use mixed brackets and incomplete citations; adopt a consistent referencing style (e.g., Harvard) throughout.
Methodology describes data sources but lacks justification for correlation tests and cost-estimate choices; explain why these methods were selected.
Limitations section notes informal employment estimation but stops abruptly; expand on potential bias and data reliability issues.
Link the epidemiological detail directly to your research question by explaining how ADE influences transmission under climatic and urban pressures.
Table 3 graph trend lacks axis labels and units; label axes clearly to enable independent interpretation.
Discussion of age-group impact notes economic loss but lacks further analysis on workforce implications; elaborate for a coherent argument.
The section on climatic anomalies introduces multiple figures but does not provide statistical analysis of correlation strength; include p-values or confidence intervals.
Figure 4 map is low resolution and scale is unclear; use higher-quality GIS data and include scale bar and north arrow.
Graph 2âs legend and axis units are omitted; add labels for PPC axes (labour units vs. output) and clarify curve shifts.
When applying the PPC model, cite a standard economics source to justify its use for labour-output analysis.
Your calculation of production losses uses seven-day outputs but does not assess sensitivity to varying illness durations; include scenario analysis.
Graph 3 on AD shifts is embedded in the text without interpretation; explicitly discuss how the shifts relate to real GDP and price levels.
Graph 4âs caption does not match the labour market diagram shown; correct or clarify the graphicâs focus.
Graph 5 is introduced with minimal analysis; deepen the discussion of cost-push inflation and potential policy responses.
The conclusion restates findings but does not directly answer the refined research question; explicitly tie conclusions back to the RQ.
Recommendations are stated generally; propose specific policies or interventions with measurable targets for vector control and urban planning.
The bibliography shows wide source use but is inconsistently formatted; unify citation style and correct journal/URL formats.
